segments.text: Hello everyone, welcome to this chapter, Phenology-guided deep learning with uncertainty quantification for soybean yield prediction.
I'm Chishan Zhang, and today I'm going to walk through with you with this chapter, which mainly covered how to use time series based deep learning models to do the crop yield prediction.
I will first introduce a little bit about introduction and the background.
So why crop yield estimation matters, especially soybean yield prediction.
So as you may already know, in the near future,
the one critical global issue is food security,
mainly because we are having a very fast growing population in the recent years.
And the soybeans among all those foods can be the main source of protein,
can be critical for the animal feed, oil production and global trade.
So it's very important to know the global soybean production.
To know that we need to do the yield prediction first.
And it's not only important for the decision making like the policy and market decision, risk management and early warning.
But also pretty important for optimizing the management practice like for farmers.
They can optimize their irrigation strategies and the planting days windows.
And for now, there are two mainstream methods to do the yield projection.
The first is process-based crop models.
Those kind of models are based on the complicated but realistic crop and soil process.
So those models can be quite accurate, but it requires extensive field-level input data.
and you need to calibrate to different regions, different crops.
So usually collecting those input data can be quite time-consuming and expensive.
So it would be very difficult to apply those kind of models to a large area.
So in another way, we have the empirical models.

So different from the process models, we're just trying to build a flexible empirical relationship, which may not complicate, which may not realistic crop and soil process, but can be quite more flexible.
like here if you look at figures just we are trying to build a relationship with NDVI and the
crop use and usually those NDVI and other environment variables they are much more
accessible so those kind of methods has been successful successfully applied to large area
especially nowadays we have the advancement of AI and deep learning models and a lot of
time-series based deep learning models can be used in these applications like
deep neural networks long short-term memory LSTM models and convolutional neural networks
they all share the similar input variables which is like the time series data of remote sensing
climate variables and soil so those models are quite powerful but they still face some challenges
especially in the crop yield modeling application so first i will call it deterministic prediction
because usually the standard deep learning models output single values like 55 Boucher curves
without the error bounds.
So like I said, the crop yield estimation usually are very important
and can be used for the decision making.
But the users cannot distinguish the high and the low confidence of those predictions.
So that could be a very big issue for the decision making.
Like for example, knowing tomorrow is 25 Celsius is far less useful without the probability of a raining.
So that's the first challenge. The second one is about the time series data itself.
Because if you use the remote sensing data or climate data,
climate data usually the traditional models aggregate those inputs by calendar weeks and months
so that's very natural when we're collecting those data but when we do the crop yield modeling
because of the crop biology crop grows based on the thermal units and soil so it's not
